Cesar Chavez, a Civil Rights Icon, Is Accused of Abusing Girls for Years
A Times investigation found extensive evidence that the United Farm Workers co-founder groomed and sexually abused young girls who worked in the movement.
My colleague Sarah Hurtes and I talked to Ana Murguia and Debra Rojas over a long period as they weighed speaking publicly for the first time about how Cesar Chavez sexually abused them as girls.

We had received a tip in 2021 ​that the civil rights leader had abused women and girls over his life, including allegations of rape. As we reported, we heard about Ms. Murguia and Ms. Rojas and contacted them.

Times journalists approach victims of abuse with care. We generally do so once we have credible information about what happened. We identify ourselves as reporters and make it clear that speaking to us is their choice.

Ms. Murguia and Ms. Rojas​ initially didn’t want to talk. We respected their pace. It is vital for reporters to give sources the time they need, especially on such sensitive stories. In time, they asked about our reporting. We listened and answered.
We hoped they would speak on the record. Using their names gives a story more credibility and authority. We wanted them to be comfortable with us, too; Sarah brought her dog to one interview.

In the end, they trusted us with their stories. Five other women also described abuse, assault and misconduct by Mr. Chavez, including Dolores Huerta, a former ally of his, who said he sexually assaulted her.
John Sotomayor/The New York Times; Cathy Murphy/Getty Images; Ariana Drehsler for The New York Times
